role_hints.oews_basis: 37-2021 Pest Control Workers 54.7% of wage bill (60.3% of employment)
role_hints.note: Licensed applicators physically treating structures on recurring routes — mature AI does not replace the visit. AI augments (route optimization, on-site pest ID from photos, service documentation) without removing the technician; smart traps/remote monitoring can reduce visit frequency on commercial accounts, which is a demand question, not a labor-substitution one.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-9061 Office Clerks 6.2% + 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 2.9% + 43-6014 Secretaries/Admin 2.0% + 43-1011 Office Supervisors 2.1% + 43-3031 Bookkeeping Clerks 1.2%
role_hints.note: Scheduling, dispatch, billing, renewal processing and inbound-call handling — the genuinely automatable core; AI phone/scheduling agents apply directly here.
Multi-branch reporting, pricing and routing analytics automate; local hiring and commercial account relationships persist.
Inspection-based selling (termite WDO reports) and door-to-door residential sales remain human; lead handling, quoting and follow-up automate.
Route supervision and training; AI widens span of control modestly.
Physical application work plus small misc tail.

source_hints.source: PCT (Pest Control Technology) magazine State of the Industry report (annual)
source_hints.why: Operator surveys covering technology adoption (routing software, AI phone agents, smart traps) and business metrics.

source_hints.source: NPMA (National Pest Management Association) member/workforce data
source_hints.why: Trade association covering the operator base; owner demographics and workforce shortage evidence.

source_hints.source: Potomac Pest Control Group M&A commentary (Paul Giannamore; 'The Buzz'/Potomac deal coverage)
source_hints.why: The dominant sell-side advisor in pest control; regularly discusses prevailing EBITDA/revenue multiples for $1-10M targets and platform activity.
Revenue rankings that quantify concentration and name the active acquirers (Rollins, Rentokil/Terminix, Anticimex, Certus and PE platforms).

source_hints.source: Specialty Products Consultants 'Strategic Analysis of the U.S. Structural Pest Control Industry' (Gary Curl annual market survey)
source_hints.why: The standard market-size and segment-mix (residential/commercial/termite) survey; confirm it is still published.

capture_questions: Pricing is recurring per-visit/quarterly or annual subscription plans set locally, with no payer, auditor or fee schedule above the operator — when AI cuts office and routing costs, does the operator keep the spread as margin, or do national brands' door-to-door pricing and Google Local Services lead auctions compete it away in residential? Split the answer by residential recurring vs commercial contract vs termite/WDO work.

Route density is the profit engine: the same AI scheduling gain is worth far more inside a dense route. In documented roll-up economics, how much of the margin uplift comes from densification and cross-selling versus back-office cost removal — and does that change what an AI-era acquirer should pay a standalone target?

Who owns the customer: recurring residential accounts attach to the brand/phone number and transfer well in acquisitions (this is why the industry rolls up), but commercial accounts (food processing, healthcare, hospitality) hinge on compliance documentation and named relationships — what churn evidence exists post-acquisition for each?

What could bypass the operator: smart-trap/remote-monitoring hardware reducing visit frequency on commercial contracts, DIY-plus-AI diagnosis at the residential low end, or pest-prevention-as-a-feature bundled by other home services — is there measurable revenue-per-account erosion anywhere yet?
terminal_value_question: Pests are eliminated by a state-licensed technician physically treating the structure — mature AI does not perform the visit, so the product survives; the automatable surface is the ~30-35% of wage bill in office, sales, supervision and management around the route. The sharp question is therefore about capture and demand at the edges: does AI shift customer acquisition power to gatekeepers (Google LSA auctions, national ad-budget brands) and does remote monitoring thin out visit frequency on commercial accounts — leaving the local operator with the same truck rolls but weaker pricing? Distinguish AI cutting the operator's overhead (real, bounded) from any mechanism by which the paid visit itself goes away (largely absent here) — and keep the two out of a single blended score.
special_notes: EBITDA normalization: sub-$5M-revenue operators are owner-operated; quoted small-deal 'multiples' are frequently SDE multiples with owner comp added back — normalize to market-rate manager comp before comparing to platform exit multiples. Termite work adds retreat/repair guarantee liabilities (long-tail obligations) that quality-of-earnings must reserve for.

n_band sensitivity: the chain's 15.65% generic margin puts only the 100-499 class (70 firms) in the $1-10M band, but well-run pest operators commonly run 20%+ EBITDA — at those margins part of the 20-99 class (766 firms, avg revenue $4.7M) enters the band. Treat n_band 70 as a floor and say so.

Consolidation is intense and well documented (Rollins, Rentokil/Terminix, Anticimex, Certus, plus PE platforms; Potomac-brokered deals) — usable only for B (consolidator count) and M (multiples); never as evidence for V or A (rule 2).
Seasonality and geography: mosquito/termite seasons make quarterly data and northern-vs-sunbelt comparisons misleading; sunbelt route density is structurally better.
Recurring-revenue share is the valuation driver — one-time jobs vs recurring plans changes the multiple more than size does; verify the mix in any comp.

Boundary: vegetation/lawn pest application overlaps 561730 (landscaping) codes; wildlife/animal removal and fumigation specialists sit inside 561710 but have different economics; state applicator licensing constrains technician supply (a labor-cost tailwind for AI-assisted retention/training, not a substitution channel).
